elements.sent-2411-001: The dynamic imbalance of the fore-background is a major challenge in video object counting, which is usually caused by the sparsity of foreground objects.
elements.sent-2411-002: This often leads to severe under- and over-prediction problems and has been less studied in existing works.
elements.sent-2411-003: To tackle this issue in video object counting, we propose a density-embedded Efficient Masked Autoencoder Counting (E-MAC) framework in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-004: To effectively capture the dynamic variations across frames, we utilize an optical flow-based temporal collaborative fusion that aligns features to derive multi-frame density residuals.
elements.sent-2411-005: The counting accuracy of the current frame is boosted by harnessing the information from adjacent frames.
elements.sent-2411-006: More importantly, to empower the representation ability of dynamic foreground objects for intra-frame, we first take the density map as an auxiliary modality to perform $\mathtt{D}$ensity-$\mathtt{E}$mbedded $\mathtt{M}$asked m$\mathtt{O}$deling ($\mathtt{DEMO}$) for multimodal self-representation learning to regress density map.
elements.sent-2411-007: However, as $\mathtt{DEMO}$ contributes effective cross-modal regression guidance, it also brings in redundant background information and hard to focus on foreground regions.
elements.sent-2411-008: To handle this dilemma, we further propose an efficient spatial adaptive masking derived from density maps to boost efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-009: In addition, considering most existing datasets are limited to human-centric scenarios, we first propose a large video bird counting dataset DroneBird, in natural scenarios for migratory bird protection.
elements.sent-2411-010: Extensive experiments on three crowd datasets and our DroneBird validate our superiority against the counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-011: Video object counting aims to estimate the number of objects in video scenes and has been used in various practical applications, from traffic management to public security.
elements.sent-2411-012: It has the potential to be used in decreasing the workload of public management and protecting migratory birds.
elements.sent-2411-013: Due to the crucial role of object counting in multiple application scenarios, it has attracted broad attention in recent years with the development of computer vision.
elements.sent-2411-014: Despite many excellent works that have been proposed over the past decades, most of these methods are based on static single-frame images~\citep{Zhang_2016_CVPR, Li_2018_CSRNet extracted from video, leading to significant loss of dynamic inter-frame information, especially for the swiftly moving targets.}
elements.sent-2411-015: In practice, such as crowd or animal activity analysis, the source data is often captured in video form by surveillance cameras or drones.
elements.sent-2411-016: Unlike static single-frame images, video data is significantly dynamic in the spatial motion variations of foreground objects across adjacent time instances, thereby providing richer contextual information.
elements.sent-2411-017: Therefore, by capturing the inter-frame information between video frames, the model is qualified to better perceive dynamical targets, thereby improving the accuracy and stability of the counting performance.
elements.sent-2411-018: To this concern, some video counting methods appeared recently~\citep{zou2019e3d, bai2021monet, Hossain_2020_MOPN , which aim to capture the dynamism between frames by employing techniques such as 3D convolutions or incorporating additional information.}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, since the video data inherently suffers from the problem of redundant background information~\cite{labatch , extracting features of dynamic targets in multiple frames may lead to an imbalance between foreground and background information, posing challenges for the model's optimization and inference.}
elements.sent-2411-020: More recently, inspired by the unprecedented strong self-representation ability of pre-trained vision foundational models~\citep{MaskedAutoencoders2021,tong2022videomae , researchers have injected these foundation models into downstream vision tasks to fully exploit their representational potential.}
elements.sent-2411-021: In this spirit, we present an Efficient Masked Autoencoder Counting (E-MAC) framework for video object counting.
elements.sent-2411-022: Our E-MAC introduced optical flow-based Temporal Collaborative Fusion ($\mathtt{TCF}$) to establish inter-frame relationships, constructing a pre-trained visual foundation model-based video counting framework.
elements.sent-2411-023: The optical flow between frames is used to warp the predicted density map of the adjacent frame to the current frame.
elements.sent-2411-024: Then, we perform cross-attention between the warped density map and the predicted current density map to get the final result.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, the high dynamics of video data often lead to imbalanced optimization of the sparse foreground.
elements.sent-2411-026: Different from most existing techniques, we take the density map as an additional auxiliary modality of images and transfer the self-representation foundation model to object counting for the first time.
elements.sent-2411-027: We constructed a $\mathtt{D}$ensity-$\mathtt{E}$mbedded $\mathtt{M}$asked m$\mathtt{O}$deling ($\mathtt{DEMO}$) that takes inputs from both the image and the density map, which performs feature interaction through the encoder and reconstructs the density map from masked image and density map.
elements.sent-2411-028: To this end, the density self-representation learning drives the regression implicitly by reconstructing the masked density maps.
elements.sent-2411-029: In addition, while the self-representation learning of density maps facilitates efficient density regression, the dynamic nature of foreground objects in video data still brings significant imbalanced challenges to optimization.
elements.sent-2411-030: Stochastic masked image modeling struggles to focus the model on extracting features from dynamic moving targets, leading to redundant background reconstruction that hinders model optimization.
elements.sent-2411-031: To handle this dilemma, we further develop a $\mathtt{S}$patial $\mathtt{A}$daptive $\mathtt{M}$asking ($\mathtt{SAM}$) to generate dynamic efficient masks.
elements.sent-2411-032: During training, $\mathtt{SAM}$ dynamically generates masks based on the correlated density map of each sample, providing valid information while filtering out redundant background details.
elements.sent-2411-033: Our framework employs a post-fusion strategy and develops a simple cross-attention module to compute the residuals between adjacent predicted density maps, and design a skip connect to add the residuals to the predicted density map of the current frame, which ultimately filters the non-dynamic objects in the background.
elements.sent-2411-034: In this paper, we validate our E-MAC not only in human-centric scenarios but also in natural scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-035: A large-scale video bird counting dataset DroneBird is collected for migratory bird protection.
elements.sent-2411-036: To the best of our knowledge, DroneBird is the first video bird counting dataset that is captured from a drone's viewpoint and provides abundant annotations and rich attributions.
elements.sent-2411-037: Experimental results on three human-centric datasets and our DroneBird dataset demonstrate the superiority of our method over the competing methods.
elements.sent-2411-038: Our main contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-039: We propose a density-embedded efficient masked autoencoder counting framework for video object counting, which integrates the foundational model and takes the density map as an auxiliary modality to perform self-representation learning, effectively driving density map regression implicitly.
elements.sent-2411-040: We propose an efficient spatial adaptive masking method to overcome the dynamic density distribution and make the model focus on the foreground regions.
elements.sent-2411-041: It adaptively generates image masks according to the corresponding density maps, effectively addressing the problem of imbalanced fore-background.
elements.sent-2411-042: We propose a large-scale bird counting dataset DroneBird for bird activities analysis.
elements.sent-2411-043: To our knowledge, DroneBird is the first video bird counting dataset.
elements.sent-2411-044: Extensive experiments on three human-centric scenarios and our DroneBird dataset validate our superiority compared to the competing methods.
elements.sent-2411-045: The vast majority of proposed object counting methods were commonly based on a single image.
elements.sent-2411-046: Existing counting methods~\citep{Li_2018_CSRNet, Liu_2019_CAN, liang2022transcrowd were mainly based on density map estimation, which generated the density map from point annotations and took it as the ground truth.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Most current counting methods tend to use density map regression as the pretext task of object counting since it provides more low-level supervision signals and is easier to optimize.
elements.sent-2411-048: Earlier researchers~\citep{Zhang_2016_CVPR, Li_2018_CSRNet explored improving convolutional neural network structures to enhance density regression performance by extracting multi-scale features from images.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Recent methods~\citep{ma2019bayesian, lin2022boosting utilized Bayesian loss for density contribution models from point labeling, improving upon density map supervision.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Additionally, researchers have integrated CNNs and Transformers to leverage the attention mechanism~\citep{tian2021cctrans, liang2022transcrowd .}
elements.sent-2411-051: More recently, some methods introduced pre-trained foundational models to build object counting methods~\citep{clipcount, vlcounter , thereby counting the number of any examples.}
elements.sent-2411-052: This inspired us to explore the visual foundation model based video object counting framework.. Video Object Counting.
elements.sent-2411-053: The single-frame image methods focus on spatial information from static images and neglect temporal processing, making it difficult to address the dynamic nature of video object counting tasks.
elements.sent-2411-054: Video object counting methods aim to leverage information from neighboring frames to enhance the estimation of the current frame.
elements.sent-2411-055: LSTM and 3D convolutions are commonly used methods for modeling temporal dependencies between frames~\citep{zou2019e3d, ConvLSTM .}
elements.sent-2411-056: Unlike these implicit methods of establishing frame associations, leveraging object movement direction and optical flow information can further enhance counting accuracy~\citep{droneanimal, hou2023frvcc .}
elements.sent-2411-057: However, existing video counting methods~\citep{Liu_2020_EPF, hou2023frvcc mainly address temporal relationships but often neglect intra-frame dynamics of foreground regions.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Additionally, the high cost of dot annotations restricts the availability of large video counting datasets, complicating effective learning of dynamic regions.
elements.sent-2411-059: Our proposed method treats counting as a density reconstruction task, incorporating self-representation learning of density maps with a dynamic spatial adaptive masking module, which significantly enhances the counting performance.. Masked Image Modeling.
elements.sent-2411-060: Masked image modeling refers to the reconstruction of the masked portion of a masked image by learning its representation.
elements.sent-2411-061: With the application of Transformer~\citep{vaswani2017attention in vision and the success of the BERT~\citep{devlin-etal-2019-bert pre-training paradigm in natural language processing in recent years, masked image modeling has achieved great progress.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: After some enlightening work~\citep{dae,igpt2020chen, bao2022beit , MAE~\citep{MaskedAutoencoders2021 chunks the image, randomly masks out the majority of the image patches and then reconstructs them, which has achieved great success on downstream tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Inspired by MAE, many works~\citep{tong2022videomae, bachmann2022multimae have begun to apply masked image prediction to diverse scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Considering the strong representation ability of visual foundation models, we attempt to embed the density map to guide the masked prediction for intra-frame, performing density-driven regression from image to density map and forming an efficient self-representation learning framework for video object counting.

elements.sent-2411-065: Video object counting methods not only hold promising application prospects in human-centric activity analysis, but also possess invaluable potential in natural scenarios, such as migratory bird protection.
elements.sent-2411-066: In the scenario of counting volant species like birds, to the best of our knowledge, the existing open-source data is largely limited to discrete image data~\citep{penguin, birdcount , which makes it challenging to apply these methods to dynamic bird activity analysis scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-067: To alleviate the issue of data scarcity as well as to assist in migratory bird activity analysis, we collected a new large-scale video bird dataset called DroneBird.
elements.sent-2411-068: DroneBird provides point annotations for bird counting, and also provides additional trajectory annotations for further bird tracking.
elements.sent-2411-069: To the best of our knowledge, DroneBird is the first bird dataset captured in video from a drone's viewpoint and provides both point annotations and trajectory annotations.
elements.sent-2411-070: We have collected statistics on various aspects of our DroneBird dataset and compared them with some existing datasets in Table.~\ref{dronebird .}
elements.sent-2411-071: All the videos in DroneBird are recorded at 30 frames per second with resolutions of $2160\times4096$ or $2160\times3840$.
elements.sent-2411-072: Each frame contains between $8$ and $673$ annotated objects, averaging $171.5$ per frame.
elements.sent-2411-073: The dataset includes $3,686,409$ bird annotations and $9,389$ bird trajectories, ranging from $1$ to $500$ frames in length.
elements.sent-2411-074: To further investigate DroneBird, we have analyzed five main attributes of each sample, i.e., Illumination, Density, Perspective, Distance, and Posture.
elements.sent-2411-075: We present the distribution of these attributes and their correlation in the Fig.~\ref{fig:chord_dronebird .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Each arc represents a attribute, and each chord connects between two arcs, indicating that there are images that possess both attributes represented by the two arcs.
elements.sent-2411-077: For each attribute, we provide two example images in DroneBird dataset for reference.
elements.sent-2411-078: Detailed descriptions of these attributes and clearer visualization are presented in Appendix~\ref{sec:append_dronebird .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{center} 1\textwidth{!}{ \includegraphics{Figures/new_framework.pdf} } \end{center} \caption{An overview of our E-MAC. For the temporal collaborative fusion, we use optical flow to fuse multi-frame density maps. For the density-embedded masked modeling, the image and density map are treated as multi-modal data and are fed into the transformer autoencoder for self-representation masked modeling simultaneously. The spatial adaptive masking uses the density map to balance the dynamic fore-background. During inference, the density map is fully masked.} \label{new_framework}
elements.sent-2411-079: In this paper, we introduce an Efficient Masked Autoencoder Counting (E-MAC) framework based on a self-representation foundation model for video object counting.
elements.sent-2411-080: The framework of our E-MAC is depicted in Fig.~\ref{new_framework , which consists of temporal collaborative fusion ($\mathtt{TCF}$), density-embedded masked modeling ($\mathtt{DEMO}$), and spatial adaptive masking ($\mathtt{SAM}$).}
elements.sent-2411-081: We utilize optical flow to establish connections across multiple frames to capture inter-frame information.
elements.sent-2411-082: A temporal residual map is constructed by leveraging optical flow information between frames, which utilizes historical data to enhance the counting performance of the current frame.
elements.sent-2411-083: For intra-frame information, we employ density-embedded masked modeling ($\mathtt{DEMO}$) and spatial adaptive masking ($\mathtt{SAM}$) based on the self-representation foundation model to effectively balance the learning on foreground and background for more accurate density map estimation.
elements.sent-2411-084: The temporal collaborative fusion aims to integrate multiple frames for more accurate estimation.
elements.sent-2411-085: Given the frames at time \(t\) and \(t-1\), each sample consists of a frame image and a density map.
elements.sent-2411-086: The samples of two frames can be described as $\mathbf{S _t = \{I_t, D_t\}$ and $\mathbf{S _{t-1 = \{I_{t-1 , D_{t-1}\}$, which are then fed into the \ema~for density-embedded masked modeling.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Different from most existing methods, we take the density map as an auxiliary modality corresponding to the image modality.
elements.sent-2411-088: Specifically, for a sample $\mathbf{S_t = \{I_t, D_t\}$, the patch embedding module patchifies and embeds both the image modality and density map modality into multi-modal tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-089: The SAM removes specific patches from these multi-modal tokens before the transformer encoder.

elements.sent-2411-091: The decoder then reconstructs the complete original density map based on the incomplete input information.

elements.sent-2411-242: This paper aims to address the dynamic imbalance of the fore-background in video object counting.
elements.sent-2411-243: Considering the dynamic sparsity of foreground objects, we proposed a density-embedded efficient masked autoencoder counting framework.
elements.sent-2411-244: We introduced the self-representation foundation model to video object counting, which first takes the density map as an auxiliary modality and develops density-embedded masked modeling ($\mathtt{DEMO}$) to drive the regression of density map estimation.
elements.sent-2411-245: To handle the infra-frame dynamic density distribution and make the model focus more on the foreground region in the self-representation learning, we proposed a simple but efficient Spatial Adaptive Masking ($\mathtt{SAM}$), which dynamically generates masks depending on density maps to eliminate the effect of redundant background information and boost the performance.
elements.sent-2411-246: Furthermore, accounting for the inter-frame dynamism and utilizing the inherent temporal information in the video, we introduce the optical flow and propose a temporal collaborative fusion that learns to harness the inter-frame differences.
elements.sent-2411-247: Besides, we first proposed a new large-scale video bird dataset in the drone perspective, named DroneBird.
elements.sent-2411-248: Our DroneBird provides point and trajectory annotations in different scenes for counting and further localization and tracking tasks.
elements.sent-2411-249: We evaluated our method on our DroneBird, the FDST dataset, the Mall dataset, and the VSCrowd dataset.
elements.sent-2411-250: The experimental results demonstrate the superiority of our framework.. \bibliographystyle{iclr2025_conference .}
elements.sent-2411-251: Our DroneBird dataset is captured by cameras mounted on drones using consumer drones such as the DJI Mavic 2 Pro, Phantom 4 Pro, etc. DroneBird captures a wide range of scenarios, including rivers, wetlands, lakes, ice, and other common bird habitats.
elements.sent-2411-252: The data captured in DroneBird is primarily obtained by the drone from 30 meters or 60 meters in the air, with a small portion of the data captured close to the ground.
elements.sent-2411-253: DroneBird's collection time is during the daytime or in the early evening when the weather conditions are favorable, and the view is relatively clear.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Framework Workflow in Training Phase} \label{algorithm_whole} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Ensure{ \{$I_{\text{t}}, D_{\text{t}}\}, \{I_{\text{t-1}},D_{\text{t-1}}\}$} \Require{$\hat{D}_{\text{fuse}}$} \ForAll{$\text{epoch}$} \State $\hat{D}_{\text{t}} \gets \phi_\mathtt{DEMO}(I_t, D_t)$ \State $\hat{D}_{\text{t-1}} \gets \phi_\mathtt{DEMO}(I_{t-1}, D_{t-1})$ \State $\mathcal{M} \gets \phi_{\text{OpticalFlow}}(I_{\text{t-1}}, I_{\text{t}})$ \State $\hat{D}_{\text{t-1}}^{\text{warp}} \gets \phi_{\text{warp}}(\mathcal{M}, \hat{D}_{\text{t-1}})$ \State $\hat{D}_{\text{t}}^{\text{res}} \gets \phi_{\text{cross-attention}}(\hat{D}_{\text{t-1}}^{\text{warp}}, \hat{D}_{\text{t}})$ \State $\hat{D}_{\text{fuse}} \gets \hat{D}_{\text{t}} + \hat{D}_{\text{t}}^{\text{res}}$ \State $I_{\text{t-1}}^{\text{warp}} \gets \phi_{\text{warp}}(\mathcal{M}, I_{\text{t-1}})$ \State $\mathcal{L}_{\text{fuse}}, \mathcal{L}_{\text{cur}}, \mathcal{L}_{\text{opt}}, \mathcal{L}_{\text{TV}} \gets \phi_{\text{Loss}}$ \State $\mathcal{L} \gets \lambda_1 \mathcal{L}_{\text{fuse}} + \lambda_2 \mathcal{L}_{\text{cur}} + \lambda_3 \mathcal{L}_{\text{opt}} + \lambda_4 \mathcal{L}_{\text{TV}}$ \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{\ema~workflow in training phase} \label{algorithm2} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Ensure{ $I, D$} \Require{$\hat{D}$} \State $\mathbf{T}_I, \mathbf{T}_D \gets \phi_\text{patchify}(I, D)$ \State $\mathbb{N} = random(0,1)$ \If{ $ \mathbb{N} \leq 1 - \mathcal{P}$} \State $\mathcal{K} \gets argsort_{des}(\mathbf{T}_D)$ \Else \State $\mathcal{K} \gets argsort_{asc}(\mathbf{T}_D)$ \EndIf \ForAll{$t_i \in \mathbf{T}_I$} \If{$i \in \mathcal{K}$} \State $M^i \gets 0$ \Else \State $M^i \gets 1$ \EndIf \EndFor \State $\mathbf{T}_I^\text{ret}, \mathbf{T}_D^\text{ret} \gets mask(\mathbf{T}_I, M), mask(\mathbf{T}_D, random)$ \State $\mathbf{T}^\text{ret} \gets \phi_{\text{concate}}(\mathbf{T}_I^\text{ret}, \mathbf{T}_D^\text{ret})$ \State $\mathbf{T}^\text{ret} \gets \phi_{\text{encoder}}(\mathbf{T}^\text{ret})$ \State $\mathbf{T}_I^\text{ret}, \mathbf{T}_D^\text{ret} \gets \text{split}(\mathbf{T}^\text{ret})$ \State $\text{Mask} = random$ \State $\hat{\mathbf{T}_D} \gets \phi_\text{fill}(\mathbf{T}_D^\text{ret}, \text{Mask})$ \State $\hat{\mathbf{T}_D} \gets \phi_\text{cross-attention}(\hat{\mathbf{T}_D}, \mathbf{T}^\text{ret})$ \State $\hat{D} \gets \phi_{\text{decoder}}(\hat{\mathbf{T}_D})$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-375: We have compiled the dataset's statistics and compared them with existing bird datasets, with the results shown in Table~\ref{dronebird .}
elements.sent-2411-376: Most of the targets in DroneBird are small in size.
elements.sent-2411-377: We compared the pixel height occupied by individual bird targets in DroneBird with that occupied by individuals in existing crowd data, and visualized this comparison in Fig.~\ref{pixel_cmp .}
elements.sent-2411-378: The pixel height occupied by individual bird targets is significantly smaller than that of crowd individuals, posing a significant challenge for the target counting task.
elements.sent-2411-379: We summarize the contributions and new challenges brought by DroneBird into the following four points:.
elements.sent-2411-380: Small target: We compared the number of foreground pixels occupied by independent targets in the DroneBird data and the existing crowd data at the same resolution, and the independent targets in DroneBird occupy fewer pixels (less than 20 pixels height) than the independent targets in the existing dataset (more than 160 pixels height).
elements.sent-2411-381: Note that although the counting target in the crowd counting task is the human head, the human body still provides important information, and the human body occupies more pixels compared to birds, so our dataset provides data on small targets for counting.
elements.sent-2411-382: On the other hand, to avoid drone interference with bird activity, DroneBird's drone was photographed farther away from the flock, resulting in a further reduction of pixels occupied by the photographed individual birds.
elements.sent-2411-383: Sparse distribution: Unlike humans, birds in nature need enough space to move around, which means birds are more sparsely distributed in space.
elements.sent-2411-384: Coupled with the smaller target size of birds, the sparseness of the foreground portion is even more pronounced in DroneBird compared to the already existing dataset.
elements.sent-2411-385: Fast movement: Unlike existing video crowd-sourced data, the unique flight movements of birds allow them to do fast movements, posing a challenge for better temporal modeling.
elements.sent-2411-386: New counting category: DroneBird fills the gap of missing video bird data in the field.
elements.sent-2411-387: The models trained on crowd data fail to conduct bird counting directly (See Table~\ref{trans_exp ).}
elements.sent-2411-388: We evaluated the performance of our method trained on the VSCrowd dataset (Exp.~1) and the DroneBird training set (Exp.~2) on the DroneBird testing set.
elements.sent-2411-389: The results show that the model trained on the VSCrowd dataset failed to perform well on the bird data, which indicates the large gap between crowd and bird data.
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Framework Workflow in Training Phase} \label{algorithm_whole} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Ensure{ \{$I_{\text{t}}, D_{\text{t}}\}, \{I_{\text{t-1}},D_{\text{t-1}}\}$} \Require{$\hat{D}_{\text{fuse}}$} \ForAll{$\text{epoch}$} \State $\hat{D}_{\text{t}} \gets \phi_\mathtt{DEMO}(I_t, D_t)$ \State $\hat{D}_{\text{t-1}} \gets \phi_\mathtt{DEMO}(I_{t-1}, D_{t-1})$ \State $\mathcal{M} \gets \phi_{\text{OpticalFlow}}(I_{\text{t}}, I_{\text{t-1}})$ \State $\hat{D}_{\text{t-1}}^{\text{warp}} \gets \phi_{\text{warp}}(\mathcal{M}, \hat{D}_{\text{t-1}})$ \State $\hat{D}_{\text{t}}^{\text{res}} \gets \phi_{\text{ca}}(\hat{D}_{\text{t-1}}^{\text{warp}}, \hat{D}_{\text{t}})$ \State $\hat{D}_{\text{fuse}} \gets \hat{D}_{\text{t}} + \hat{D}_{\text{t}}^{\text{res}}$ \State $\mathcal{L}_{\text{fuse}}, \mathcal{L}_{\text{cur}}, \mathcal{L}_{\text{opt}}, \mathcal{L}_{\text{TV}} \gets \phi_{\text{Loss}}$ \State $\mathcal{L} \gets \lambda_1 \mathcal{L}_{\text{fuse}} + \lambda_2 \mathcal{L}_{\text{cur}} + \lambda_3 \mathcal{L}_{\text{opt}} + \lambda_4 \mathcal{L}_{\text{TV}}$ \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{\ema~workflow in training phase} \label{algorithm2} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Ensure{ $I, D$} \Require{$\hat{D}$} \State $\mathbf{T}_I, \mathbf{T}_D, \mathbf{I}_\text{patch},\mathbf{D}_\text{patch} \gets \phi_\text{patchify}(I, D)$ \State $\mathbf{V}_D \gets \phi_\text{sum}(\mathbf{D}_\text{patch})$ \State $\mathbb{N} = random(0,1)$ \If{ $ \mathbb{N} \leq 1 - \mathcal{P}$} \State $\mathcal{K} \gets argsort_{des}(\mathbf{V}_D)$ \Else \State $\mathcal{K} \gets argsort_{asc}(\mathbf{V}_D)$ \EndIf \ForAll{$t_i \in \mathbf{T}_I$} \If{$i \in \mathcal{K}$} \State $M^i \gets 0$ \Else \State $M^i \gets 1$ \EndIf \EndFor \State $\mathbf{T}_I^\text{ret}, \mathbf{T}_D^\text{ret} \gets mask(\mathbf{T}_I, M_\text{adaptive}), mask(\mathbf{T}_D, M_\text{random})$ \State $\mathbf{T}^\text{ret} \gets \phi_{\text{concate}}(\mathbf{T}_I^\text{ret}, \mathbf{T}_D^\text{ret})$ \State $\mathbf{T}^\text{ret} \gets \phi_{\text{encoder}}(\mathbf{T}^\text{ret})$ \State $\mathbf{T}_I^\text{ret}, \mathbf{T}_D^\text{ret} \gets \text{split}(\mathbf{T}^\text{ret})$ \State $\text{Mask} = random$ \State $\hat{\mathbf{T}_D} \gets \phi_\text{fill}(\mathbf{T}_D^\text{ret}, \text{Mask})$ \State $\hat{\mathbf{T}_D} \gets \phi_\text{ca}(\hat{\mathbf{T}_D}, \mathbf{T}^\text{ret})$ \State $\hat{D} \gets \phi_{\text{decoder}}(\hat{\mathbf{T}_D})$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-390: The training process of \ema~is represented in Algorithm~\ref{algorithm2 .}
elements.figure-2411-019: 1\columnwidth{!}{ \includegraphics{Figures/sam_detailed.pdf} } \caption{The detailed process of SAM. For ease of expression, we crop the image and the density map into 25 patches, as shown in the figure, this number varies according to the size of input images (each patch is set to $16\times16$). We first calculate the $\mathbf{V}_D^i$ of each density map patch $\mathbf{D}_{patch}^i$, and sort the token according to the number of targets $\mathbf{V}_D^i$. To balance the fore- and back-ground information, we set a background retention probability (BRP) $\mathcal{P}$ to determine the sorting manner, which is detailed in Sec.~\ref{sam}. For tokens from image modality, we keep the first $\mathcal{N}_I^\text{ret}$ tokens after sorting. For tokens from density map modality, we randomly shuffle their order and keep the first $\mathcal{N}_I^\text{ret}$ tokens, i.e., randomly select $\mathcal{N}_I^\text{ret}$ density tokens. Since the masked tokens are filled by learnable tokens in the decoder, we first restore the retained image and density tokens to their original order before sorting. Then, we concatenate and feed them into the encoder. Note that the restoration follows the same setting with \cite{bachmann2022multimae}, which can be performed in the decoder as well. The full set of retained tokens and filled density map tokens are then fed into the decoder to predict the density map. Specifically, the full set of retained tokens are treated as $key$ and $value$ vectors, and the filled density map tokens are treated as $query$ vector in the cross-attention layer in the decoder. Then, the output of the cross-attention layer is fed into two self-attention layers to perform the final prediction. } \label{fig:sam_detail}
